meta.concept: Hydrogen bonding, Intermolecular interactions, and physical properties

layer_1_hard_formulas.used_for: Comparing H-bond energy with covalent bonds (~400 kJ/mol) and Van der Waals forces (~2 to 5 kJ/mol).

layer_1_hard_formulas.formula: Boiling Point ∝ Magnitude of Intermolecular Forces
layer_1_hard_formulas.used_for: Explaining the abnormally high boiling points of H2O, HF, and NH3 relative to their group hydrides.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Primary Condition: H must be covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N).
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Identifying the existence of a Hydrogen bond in a molecule.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Intramolecular H-bonding: Occurs within the same molecule, typically forming 5 or 6-membered chelate rings.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Explaining properties of o-nitrophenol or salicylaldehyde.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Intermolecular H-bonding: Occurs between different molecules of the same or different substances.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Explaining high viscosity, surface tension, and boiling points.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Water-Solubility Logic: A substance is soluble in water if it can form H-bonds with water molecules.
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Predicting solubility of alcohols, carboxylic acids, and amines in water.

layer_2_rule_based_logics.rule: Strength Trend: Strength of H-bond increases with the electronegativity of the atom attached to H (F-H...F > O-H...O > N-H...N).
layer_2_rule_based_logics.applied_when: Comparing H-bond strengths across different species.

layer_3_derived_consequences.consequence: H2O is a liquid at room temperature while H2S is a gas because oxygen can form H-bonds while sulfur cannot.
Ice has a lower density than liquid water because H-bonding creates an open cage-like structure with voids.
o-Nitrophenol is steam volatile and has a lower boiling point than p-nitrophenol due to intramolecular H-bonding reducing intermolecular association.
Acetic acid exists as a dimer in benzene or vapor phase, doubling its observed molecular weight.
HF has a lower boiling point than H2O despite F being more electronegative than O, because H2O forms 4 H-bonds per molecule while HF forms only 2.
The solubility of alcohols decreases as the length of the non-polar alkyl chain increases, despite the presence of the H-bonding -OH group.
Glycerol is highly viscous because it contains three -OH groups capable of extensive intermolecular H-bonding.
The KHF2 salt exists (containing the [F-H-F]- ion), but KHCl2 does not exist because Chlorine cannot form strong H-bonds.
DNA double helix structure is stabilized by H-bonding between specific base pairs (A-T and G-C).
Para-nitrophenol has higher solubility in water than ortho-nitrophenol because the para-isomer can form intermolecular H-bonds with water.
In the series NH3, PH3, AsH3, SbH3, NH3 has the highest boiling point due to H-bonding, then the trend increases from PH3 to SbH3 due to Van der Waals forces.
Extensive H-bonding in proteins leads to the formation of alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet secondary structures.
